X3.1.label: Question X3.1
X3.1.scenario: The supervisory authorities of insurance business are reviewing the regulatory framework for health and care insurance companies and the accounting procedures to apply to these companies in their territory.
X3.1.synopsis: Question on the criteria for a new regulatory framework, ideal accounting procedures, and how professional guidance benefits health and care insurers.

(i).question: Outline the criteria that the authorities may use when deciding upon the revised plans for the regulatory framework.

Protect policyholders: The aim should be to install a system that provides a satisfactory level of protection for policyholders. [½]
The system should concentrate on those who are most vulnerable, eg private policyholders, the elderly or the infirm. [½]
Minimise cost of compliance: The system should not incur great expense for either policyholders or taxpayers. [½]
Simplicity: The system should be simple, so that insurers do not face a massive administrative burden (which will add to costs) ... [½]
... and so that policyholders are clear about the level of protection provided. [½]
Unbiased: The system should not distort the natural mechanisms of a freely competitive market, as this might lead to inefficiencies (and potentially greater cost for the policyholder). [1]
Objective: The system should have clearly-defined rules that are not open to subjectivity. [½]
Prospective: The system should look ahead, ie be able to identify and solve problems before they occur. [½]
Realistic / accurate: The system should be accurate in its diagnoses, eg it should not: [½]
— force the closure of a sound insurer [½]
— allow insolvent companies to write more business and get further into trouble [½]
Manageable: The system should not demand too much resource from the regulators. [½]
The transitional arrangements from the existing system should be designed to minimise disruption. [½]

(ii).question: Describe the ideal features of the accounting procedures.

1. Not overly complex: 1. The new procedures should not be overly complex. [½]
— can result in mistakes and misinterpretations [½]
2. Consistent: 2. The procedures should be consistent, both between companies and over time. [½]
This will allow comparisons to be made between companies and to assess trends within a company. [½]
This consistency should be both in terms of methodology and assumptions. [½]
Any changes to either of these should be justified and documented thoroughly. [½]
3. Good balance: 3. The procedures should have a good balance between freedom and prescription: [½]
— Freedom allows flexibility to make sure the accounts fairly represent the financial situation of the company under varying circumstances. [½]
— Prescription helps to avoid manipulation of the situation to achieve a desired result. [½]
When deciding this balance, the procedures should ensure that subjective areas are minimal. [½]
4. Produced regularly: 4. Accounts should be produced regularly but not too frequently. [½]
Annual accounts are reasonable for most classes of health business unless there is specific reason to require more frequent analysis (for instance, if solvency is low or for new companies). [½]
5. Appropriate detail: 5. The accounts need to have appropriate detail. If they are: [½]
— too consolidated, they will be fairly useless to a lot of interested parties [½]
— too detailed, they will take too long to produce and may be open to ambiguity. [½]
In particular, the accounts should split the long-term health business results from the short-term health business results, because the two have quite different characteristics. [½]
6. Appropriate data: 6. The data available to produce the accounts must be appropriate, and be in sufficient volume and detail to support the level of detail required in the accounts. [1]
It should be possible to produce the accounts readily from existing data systems with minimal extra effort. [½]
7. Not overly onerous: 7. The procedures should not be overly onerous or penal, so that companies would feel happy to produce them and not worry about third parties' interpretations of them. [1]
8. Desired principles: — be consistent with any other desired accounting principles, eg have the right balance between prudence and fair value [1]
— be consistent with the procedures used in other countries and with international accounting standards [½]
— bear in mind that there will be different purposes for the accounts, such as taxation, shareholders' interests, policyholders' interests and internal management interests. [1]
This will possibly mean that more than one set of accounts will be needed, because each party will probably have a different objective. [½]
9. Supported by commentary: 9. The numerical accounts should also be supported by a commentary in the context of the business, ... [½]
... so that users of the accounts do not misinterpret them. [½]

(iii).question:
Describe how the professional guidance issued by the following bodies, in relation to the role of actuaries, is beneficial to health and care insurance companies:
(a) the Institute and Faculty of Actuaries to its members [7]
(b) the Financial Reporting Council. [4]

(a) Institute and Faculty of Actuaries: The Institute and Faculty of Actuaries issue the following guidance to members:
— The Actuaries' Code
— Actuarial Professional Standards
— Other non-mandatory guidance. [1½]
The Actuaries' Code
The Actuaries' Code is focussed on the ethical considerations that actuaries employed by health and care insurers should follow within any work that might reflect upon the actuarial profession. [½]
For a health and care insurer, this will ensure that actuaries employed within the organisation will carry out their roles with a high-level of professionalism. [½]
In particular, the Actuaries' Code would ensure any actuaries employed by the insurer displayed:
— integrity - acting honestly in the work carried out for the insurer. [½]
— competence and care - ensuring they have the required skills to carry out their role within the insurance company [½]

— good communication - open and clear communication with all those that they work with, both within the insurer and with any external parties (eg reinsurers, investment managers), ensuring that the communication is appropriate for the audience. [½]
— impartiality - not being 'swayed' on a particular course of action without objective evidence, eg not recommending a particular reinsurer because of a personal acquaintance. [½]
Any areas where there is a risk of a potential conflict of interest should be flagged with the insurer so that appropriate mitigation steps can be put in place. [½]

There is also a requirement around compliance, ensuring all work undertaken complies with the relevant regulations and guidance. This will reduce the risk for the insurer of fines that may be incurred for non-compliance. [½]

The final requirement is around speaking-up should an issue be identified, either within the insurer, or to an independent body if that is not possible. This will give the insurer confidence that there are no unreported issues within their organisation that could lead to regulatory or reputational issues. [½]
Actuarial Profession Standards (APSs)
APS L1 around the duties and responsibilities of Life Assurance Actuaries' would ensure that the insurer's Chief Actuary fulfilled all the requirements of this role. [½]
APS X2 around the reviewing of actuarial work, would give the insurer confidence that the work produced by its actuaries is of a high standard and suitably checked. [½]
This should give the insurer confidence that they have carried out all the duties expected of them and no area has been overlooked. [½]

All actuaries working for the health and care insurer would be expected to comply with APS L2 around the obligation to 'whistleblow'. This would achieve a similar goal the 'speaking out' requirement of the Actuaries' Code above, in ensuring that no issues were being hidden or unreported. [½]
Other non-mandatory guidance
The IFoA also publish non-mandatory guidance on:
— speaking up for actuaries - this should reassure the insurer that any issues within its business will be identified and raised in an appropriate way [½]
— conflicts of interest - this would ensure that any conflicts of interest within the insurers business were identified and managed appropriately [½]
— ethical data science - provides assurances that actuaries will also apply all professional and ethical guidance to the handling of data within their work. [½]
[Maximum 7]
(b) Financial Reporting Council: The Financial Reporting Council produce a set of Technical Actuarial Standards (TASs) which actuaries working for the insurer will be expected to comply with. [½]
If the actuarial work carried out within the insurer is within the FRC's 'UK Geographic Scope' it will be compulsory for its actuaries to comply with these standards. [½]

The Framework for the TASs sets out the authority, scope and application of the TASs. The intention is that it ensures that users to actuarial information are able to rely on it, that it is complete and explains any limitations. [½]
This will be a very important requirement for the insurer as it is likely to base information it makes public on reports prepared by its actuaries. [½]
It will also ensure that the information is able to be used for internal decision-making in order to achieve the aims of the insurer. [½]
The guidance includes a generic TAS (TAS 100) which will be applicable to all areas of technical actuarial work carried out by the insurer. [½]

TAS 100 sets out six high-level principles that should promote high quality technical actuarial work. For the health and care insurer this should again give confidence in the work that is being carried out and the extent to which it can be relied on within the business. [½]

TAS 100 includes requirements around proportionality and materiality. This means that the actuaries should not carry out excessive levels of work where it is not proportional to the materiality of the results. This should reassure the insurer that work is being done efficiently and to the extent required by the business involved. [½]

The actuaries working for the health and care insurer will also be expected to follow the requirements of TAS 200: Insurance. The intention of this is to promote high quality technical actuarial work on insurance matters where the FRC has identified a high degree of risk to the public interest. [½]

X3.2.label: Question X3.2
X3.2.scenario: A composite insurance company transacts a range of conventional long-term and short-term health insurance business. It operates in a country outside of the EU. The regulations are currently being reviewed with a view to introducing similar regulations to those under Solvency II.
X3.2.synopsis: Comprehensive question on policy information, internal models, correlations spanning equity, persistency, morbidity, longevity, and financial constraints from Pillar 1 of Solvency II.

(i).question: List the policy information the insurer would require in order to perform a supervisory valuation of liabilities for this business.

Policy information: number of people covered (or more details of additional people covered, such as age and gender, if available) [½]
office premium and any extra premium [½]
product details (eg deferred period, excess, hospital band) [½]
date that policy was taken out [½]
current status, eg healthy, sick but not claiming, claiming [½]
if claiming, need details of claim, eg date and cause of claim [½]
(possibly) occupation, location (if basis used has this detail) [½]

(ii).question: Describe the checks that would be performed to verify the accuracy of this information.

Checks: spot checks, eg check sum insured or benefit levels for certain specified policies [½]
check that the data is complete, with as little as possible missing [½]
check maximum / minimum / average sum insured, benefit levels, age, term, etc to ensure that they are reasonable [½]
compare total policies with last time and reconcile the 'ons' and 'offs', ie current business in force should equal last time's plus new business less leavers, ... [½]
... if possible do this for number of contracts, premiums and benefit levels [½]
if possible check the movements with other sources that are available, eg accounting information [½]
analyse new business rates, withdrawal or renewal rates, etc to check they are reasonable [½]
compare with data for other valuations if available, eg for embedded value [½]
an analysis of surplus might highlight some errors [½]

(iii).question:
Under the new regime, companies are given the option to calculate their Solvency Capital Requirement (SCR) by either using:
- Standard formulae that will be set by the regulator.
OR
- An 'internal model' that will be individually designed and developed by companies that opt for this option. The model must be individually approved by the regulator. Although most companies have been using some form of model under the current solvency capital requirement regime, the regulator has indicated that any existing models are NOT likely to be good enough to be used as internal models.

Discuss the advantages and disadvantages for companies that opt for the 'internal model' approach.

Advantages: The insurers can tailor the internal model specifically to the risk profile of business. [½]
However, the standard formula will lack flexibility to allow it to adapt to the range of risk profiles of the insurers within the country. [½]
The standardised approach will most likely be calibrated conservatively. [½]
Therefore, internal models could produce lower capital requirements. [½]
This would have advantages for insurers in capital management and pricing. [½]
The development of internal models is likely to improve risk modelling and provide additional insights in risk profile for insurers. [½]
This should give those firms that use them a competitive advantage. [½]
An internal model is likely to be an important part of most companies' risk management framework (ie it helps to embed a risk management culture). [½]
Internal model may be based on existing model so may reduce training requirements and costs compared to introducing a new capital modelling approach. [½]
Requiring regulatory approval gives credibility to internal management information that the model will produce, ... [½]
... ie it should ensure senior management buy-in to the output and use it in management decisions. [½]
Disadvantages: It will take significant time to develop an internal model, which will add to costs and cause strain on resources. [½]
The insurers may not have the required expertise to build any model in-house. This may add costs for the insurer if external advice is required eg from consultants. [½]
Regulatory approval can take a long time if there is a backlog of initial applications. [½]
There may also be minimum requirement of its use within the company that will need to be complied with to demonstrate the model is used within the insurer's business. [½]
Any internal model will need to be reviewed regularly. Both internal and external reviews may be required. [½]
Producing an internal model could be a particular strain for smaller companies. [½]
The benefit of an internal model could be marginally lower or even higher calculation requirement. [½]
The designs and standards of model could diverge widely from one company to another. [½]
Having spent the upfront costs, the model might not be accepted by the regulator. [½]
An internal model could prove difficult to explain to senior management / board. [½]
It could also be harder for external analysts (eg credit rating agents) to compare between companies. [½]
It may be difficult to set calibration of risk stresses specific to the particular company ... [½]
... and similarly the correlations between risks, ... [½]
... particularly how correlations behave under stressed conditions. [½]
Insurers may not have good data on which to base an internal model, for example for operational risks. [½]
Internal models may need to get reapproved regularly (eg when a new product is launched). [½]

(iv).question: Outline the key requirements that the regulator might impose on a company that decides to design and develop its own 'internal model'.

Requirements of an internal model: Any internal model should be widely used within the company, eg pricing, reserving etc. [½]
Where possible a single model should be used throughout the business, or at least models that are consistent. [½]
The internal model must play an important role in risk management and decision-making. [½]
The design must be based on sound actuarial and statistical techniques. [½]
The data used in the model should be accurate, complete and appropriate and reflective of the business. [½]
The model should cover all material risks. [½]
It must be able to produce analysis of causes and sources of profits and losses. [½]
The company must be able to demonstrate how the categorisation of risk is carried out. [½]
There must be regular reviews of the model and a process for regular re-validation. [½]
The company must avoid over-reliance on external model providers. [½]
New users should be able to understand the operation of the model relatively easily, ie no black boxes. [½]
The model should have the ability to look at various risk measures and levels of confidence required by regulation (eg VaR = 99.5%). [½]
There should be detailed breakdown of risk including: [½]
— market risk
— interest rate risk
— credit / reinsurance risk
— operational risk
— mortality and morbidity risk
— persistency risk
— expenses risk
— risks attaching to the firm's pension scheme
— liquidity risks
— group risk
[½ for each 2 examples]
The model will require comprehensive documentation which should cover: [½]
— theory
— design
— assumptions
— results
— operation
— compliance
— shortcomings / approximations
— future developments.
[½ for each 2 examples]
The regulator may:
— specify calibration of the model (eg minimum shock tests/specified correlation assumptions) [½]
— require the use of stochastic models in some circumstances [½]
— specify treatment of new business. [½]
The regulator may also require regular reviews of the model after the initial approval. [½]
Staff who work directly with the model should be thoroughly trained. [½]
There should also be a reasonable understanding of the model by the Board and senior management. [½]
The model must be able to produce formal, detailed reconciliation of results. [½]
There should be processes in place for the internal review of the model. [½]
Processes should be in place to keep models up to date. [½]
Independent external review should be carried out. [½]
The model should be signed off by internal and/or external auditors. [½]
It may also require sign-off by a nominated individual (eg Chief Actuary). [½]
The regulator might specify:
— a minimum level of confidence to be achieved in specified time period. [½]
— a minimum capital requirement [½]
— deadlines / time limits for approval applications. [½]
The regulator may disallow switch back to formula approach if have an approved model (to avoid capital arbitrage). [½]
The regulator may also require submission of a comparison with the formula approach. [½]
[Maximum 12]

(v).question:
The regulator is considering the correlations that would be appropriate to be used under its standard formula approach to calculate the SCR. It has developed the following matrix for use by life insurance companies and is hoping it can also be used by health and care insurers. It has substituted mortality risk for morbidity risk in the matrix. Morbidity risk relates to critical illness insurance claim rates:
Equity Credit Longevity Morbidity Persistency Expense Operational
Equity 1 0.75 0 0 0.5 0 0.5
Credit 0.75 1 0 0 0.25 0 0.5
Longevity 0 0 1 -0.25 0.25 0 0.25
Morbidity 0 0 -0.25 1 0 0 0.25
Persistency 0.5 0.25 0.25 0 1 0.25 0.5
Expense 0 0 0 0 0.25 1 0.25
Operational 0.5 0.5 0.25 0.25 0.5 0.25 1
Explain the likely rationale for the following correlations and whether they are appropriate for health and care insurers: (a) equity and persistency +0.5 (b) persistency and operational +0.5 (c) persistency and morbidity 0 (or -0.25) (d) longevity and morbidity -0.25

(a) Equity and persistency 0.5: The positive correlation means that there is assumed to be a connection between equity markets falling and persistency being worse than expected (ie higher lapse rates). [½]
The fact that the correlation is 0.5 means that the regulator is suggesting that lapse rates will be impacted by a large extent but not fully, by an equity shock. [½]

This may be reflecting that the economic circumstances (times of recession or economic instability), which give rise to equity falls, may lead to higher lapses if customers can no longer afford to pay for insurance premiums, which seems reasonable. [½]

However, the link between equity markets and persistency would be more direct for unit-linked life assurance business, as a fall in equity values would affect policyholder's investments, and so a lower correlation might be more appropriate for health insurers than life insurers. [½]
(b) Persistency and operational +0.5: Higher lapses may lead to a burden on the operational area and so increase the risks within this area. [½]
For example, the customer service area dealing with lapses, or the IT systems coping with the volume of lapses. [½]
Equally it may be that operational issues such as administrative failures may lead to dissatisfied customers and higher lapses. This may have been reported in the media. [½]
In addition, management may be distracted by operational problems leading to a lack of focus on the business which in turn leads to lapses. [½]
Therefore a positive correlation is sensible, but as there are many other drivers behind both, the size of the correlation may want to be further discussed. [½]
A higher correlation may be more appropriate for health and care insurers compared to life insurers as the potential for operational issues to lead to dissatisfied policyholders may be greater. [½]
For example, speedy claim settlement for PMI is more important than speedy processing of a single premium pension contribution. [½]
(c) Persistency and morbidity 0: The regulator is assuming that the stresses are completely uncorrelated. [½]
+ve: For CI insurance there will be an affect from selective withdrawals where morbidity rates increase as lapses increase. [½]
-ve: If there are tiered benefits offered on the product (ie multiple claims are possible), persistency may be better where morbidity experience is higher. [½]
So, it may be difficult to say whether the correlation should be positive or negative, however a slight positive correlation may feel more appropriate here. [½]
(d) Longevity and morbidity -0.25: The negative correlation means that longevity risk is expected to reduce (policyholders are expected to have shorter life expectancies) at the same time as there is an increase in morbidity rates. [½]
For example, poor diets and lifestyles in a population could lead to an increase in CI claims (eg due to cancer or heart attack) and also a reduction in life expectancy of older lives. [½]
This may be the case if the factor that leads to the increase in CI claims extends to the population of lives exposed to the longevity risk too. [½]

It could be that the reasons for the morbidity shock will impact all lives, for example due to an epidemic. This could also lead to a reduction in life expectancy being experienced at the same time, although in a different population. [½]

In this situation, the two populations are not directly linked, as both rates refer to current policyholders. It is unlikely these groups will be linked as CI insurance is usually purchased by younger lives, and LTCI by older lives. [½]

Any action that reduces morbidity experience (eg medical advances that lead to early detection of cancer before a claim is triggered) could be expected to have a corresponding increase in longevity experience. [½]
Given these factors, the negative correlation between these groups may be appropriate. [½]

The regulator could consider adding a factor for longevity improvements, so that an impact on current CI claim rates would improve future longevity experience as that cohort moves through the health and care insurance system. [½]

(vi).question: Discuss the extent to which the Pillar 1 regulations under Solvency II impose financial constraints on the insurer.

Capital: To avoid increasingly severe supervisory authority action, the insurer will be constrained to hold sufficient capital to cover its Solvency Capital Requirement (SCR). [½]
The SCR is a Value at Risk measure based on a 99.5% confidence interval of the variation over one year of the amount of 'basic own funds' (broadly assets minus technical provisions). [½]
There is a prescribed list of risk groups that the SCR has to cover:
— non-life underwriting risk
— life underwriting risk
— health underwriting risk
— market risk
— counterparty default risk
— operational risk.
[½ for each 2 examples]
The insurer is constrained to hold sufficient capital to cover its Minimum Capital Requirement (MCR) to avoid losing its authorisation. [½]
The MCR is defined as a simple factor-based linear formula. [½]

Solvency II defines an 'SLT health' contract as one that is deemed to be written on a similar technical basis as life insurance (ie long-term business). A 'non-SLT health' contract is defined as one that is technically similar to non-life insurance (ie short-term business). [½]
For SLT health (and life) insurance business, the MCR formula is based on technical provisions and capital at risk on death or disability, multiplied by specified factors. [½]
For non-SLT health (and non-life) insurance business, the MCR formula is based on technical provisions and written premiums, with factors varying by product type. [½]
Restrictions are placed on the quality of capital that can be used to cover the MCR and SCR. For example: [½]
— the SCR must be backed by at least 50% Tier 1 capital and less than 15% Tier 3 [½]
— the MCR must be backed by at least 80% Tier 1 capital and no Tier 3 capital. [½]
Tier 1 capital is of the highest quality and is the most loss absorbent and permanent form of capital (eg paid-up ordinary share capital). [½]
Tier 3 capital is of the lowest quality in terms of loss absorbency and permanence (eg subordinated debt). [½]
This will place constraints on the types of capital that the insurer will want to raise, eg raising capital through a rights issue will be preferable to issuing subordinated debt. [½]
Fund structure: Constraints apply to the calculation of the SCR if there are ring-fenced funds with limitations on capital fungibility. [½]
Therefore, the insurer may choose to restructure its funds to improve its capital position. [½]
Asset selection: Solvency II requires that assets are chosen according to the 'prudent person principle'. [½]
This requires companies to develop their own set of key risk indicators to be used in making investment decisions, rather than depending on risk assessments performed by third parties. [½]
A company needs to assess its ability to manage any non-standard asset classes before investing in them. [½]
It must also have strong monitoring and control procedures in place in respect of any investments that are not traded on a regulated market. [½]
The prudent person principle also requires:
— the assets to be appropriate for the nature and term of the liabilities [½]
— assets to be chosen in the best interests of policyholders [½]
— derivatives to be used only for reducing risk or efficient portfolio management [½]
— assets to be 'properly diversified', eg by asset class, issuer, geographical area. [½]
The insurer needs to hold technical provisions consisting of a best estimate liability and a risk margin. [½]
The best estimate liability is the present value of expected future cashflows, discounted using a 'risk-free' yield curve. [½]
So, in this sense, the best estimate liability is independent of the assets held, and this rule does not constrain the choice of assets. [½]
However, the insurer may be able to make one of the following adjustments to the calculation of the risk-free rate:
— matching adjustment [½]
— volatility adjustment [½]

Where insurers have long-term predictable liabilities, and can hold matching assets to maturity, they are not exposed to the risk of changing spreads on these assets (they are not exposed to the liquidity component of spread risk, although they are still exposed to default risk). [½]

In such cases, insurers are allowed to make a matching adjustment to the risk-free discount rate by increasing it to take credit for the extra return offered by corporate bonds (over government bonds) that relates to the illiquidity premium. [1]

To take advantage of this matching adjustment the insurer must follow strict requirements in relation to its investment strategy, eg it will be constrained to hold eligible bonds until redemption. [½]
Where insurers have liabilities that are not eligible for use of the 'matching adjustment', they can alternatively add a 'volatility adjustment' to the risk-free discount rate. [½]
The purpose of the volatility adjustment is to reduce the risk of forced sales of assets in the event of extreme bond spread movements. [½]
So the existence of the volatility adjustment may reduce the constraints on investment in corporate bonds. [½]
However, the use of the volatility adjustment is subject to certain risk management requirements, such as a liquidity plan. [½]
So to take advantage of the volatility adjustment, the insurer may be constrained to hold a certain amount of liquid assets. [½]
The SCR calculation may also influence asset selection. [½]
For the market risk module, the standard formula approach involves the calculation of a series of stresses for: [½]
— interest rate
— equity
— property
— credit spread
— currency
— concentration
[½ for each 2 examples]
So, the insurer may have a preference for assets which produce a lower stress. [½]
The individual risk capital amounts are then combined across the risks within the module, using a specified correlation matrix and matrix multiplication. [½]
So, the insurer may have a preference for holding a diversified portfolio of assets as the asset classes are not perfectly correlated within the correlation matrix. [½]
Similarly, the insurer will consider the impact of its choice of assets on the size of its SCR if it uses an internal model. [½]
Contract design: A diversified mix of business may reduce the SCR which may constrain the insurer's ability to specialise on a single line of business or on niche markets. [½]
The ability to offer options and guarantees may be constrained as these would increase the size of the technical provisions. [½]
Reinsurance: The insurer should consider the probability of default when choosing its reinsurer... [½]
... as reinsurance recoveries, less an adjustment for the best estimate of expected losses due to reinsurer default, are included as an asset in the Solvency II balance sheet. [½]

(vii).question: The insurer's management is concerned about the ability of the company to meet the potential solvency capital requirement of Solvency II. Outline the steps the management might take to ease this concern, commenting on the suitability of each of the steps.

Steps a company may take to ensure it me: value of assets - value of technical provisions > SCR
The points are not necessarily in order of importance. They are grouped according to which component of the inequality above they can affect.
Increase the value of assets - valuation: Possible step: Ensure that all assets meet the prudent person principle. [½]
Suitable: Very suitable if some assets do not currently meet this principle. Should have immediate impact. [½]
Not suitable: If assets are being sold in a depressed market or if what is held gives much better returns. [½]
Possible step: Switch to reinsurers with lower probability of default. [½]
Suitable: Suitable if future reinsurance recoveries included in the assets are being reduced significantly for expected losses due to default. [½]
Not suitable: If default risk is very small or if reinsurance premiums would increase significantly. [½]
Increase the value of assets - improved: Suitable: Should improve margins over the longer term. [½]

Not suitable: No immediate impact, and will not affect existing long-term business on guaranteed terms. Not appropriate if company's premiums are already uncompetitive or if it would make them so. [½]
Suitable: Should improve margins over the longer term. [½]
Not suitable: May not have an immediate impact. Not appropriate if there are few excess costs that could be reduced. [½]
Possible step: More stringent underwriting or claims settlement. [½]
Not suitable: Extra underwriting and claim investigation can be expensive and may either directly (by underwriting) or indirectly (by causing ill will on claims) reduce levels of business. [1]
Possible step: Pull out of loss-making classes of business and/or move into more profitable lines. [1]
Not suitable: Difficult to enter the market later; may lose goodwill from policyholders who are unable to renew and insurance cycle may be turning upwards. [½]
Increase the value of assets - raise cap: Possible step: If proprietary, request more money from shareholders (rights issue) or parent company. If mutual, demutualise and raise capital through issue of shares. [1]
Suitable: If share prices are generally high, and if a good case for future profitability can be sustained. [½]
Not suitable: Otherwise. Shareholders not keen on bailing out poor companies. [½]
Suitable: If the limit of 15% Tier 3 capital for the SCR has not already been reached and debt repayments are low. [½]
Suitable: If partner with strong balance sheet and complementary business can be found. [½]
Not suitable: If a suitable partner can't be found on good terms. [½]
Reduce the value of technical provisions: Possible step: Increase risk-free rate using matching adjustment. [½]
Suitable: If insurer has long-term predictable liabilities and can hold matching assets to maturity. [½]
Not suitable: Otherwise. Imposes strict requirements on investment strategy. [½]
Possible step: Increase risk-free rate using volatility adjustment. [½]
Suitable: If insurer has liabilities that are not eligible for use of the matching adjustment. [½]
Not suitable: Imposes risk management requirements, such as a liquidity plan and sensitivity analysis. [½]
Reduce the SCR: Possible step: Sell assets with the most onerous stresses in the SCR market module calculation, eg switch from corporate bonds to government bonds to reduce credit spread. [½]
Suitable: Suitable if insurer currently follows a risky investment strategy. [½]
Not suitable: If assets are being sold in a depressed market or if we introduce a critical mismatch compared to the nature of liabilities. [½]
Possible step: Hold a more diversified portfolio of assets. [½]
Suitable: Suitable if insurer can take advantage of a lower correlation between its assets. [½]
Not suitable: If assets are being sold in a depressed market or if more risky assets are going to be held (which would offset the benefits of the step above). [½]
Possible step: Reduce guarantees and/or introduce reviewable premiums and benefits. [½]
Suitable: Should reduce capital requirements from the insurance modules, hence improve margins over the longer term. [½]
Not suitable: No immediate impact and will not be possible on existing long-term business. May lose goodwill from customers and brokers. [½]
Possible step: Sell a greater range of insurance contracts, including life and non-life. [½]
Suitable: Suitable if insurer can take advantage of a lower correlation between its business lines. [½]
Not suitable: If the insurer lacks expertise in these new lines and if these markets are unprofitable. [½]
Possible step: Increased use of non-proportional reinsurance. [½]
Suitable: Possibly as it will reduce losses from large claim amounts or high claim frequency and so could reduce the SCR from the insurance modules. [½]
Not suitable: If cost of reinsurance is high in relation to the cover provided. [½]
